metadata.title: Why the Tumor That Shrank in Mice Won't Shrink in Patients
metadata.purpose: vox-explainer slate cut of Cancer Nanomedicine card 18. Source chapter: cancer-nanomedicine/chapters/12-clinical-strategy-and-the-gap-book.md. Mechanism: subcutaneous mouse xenograft is an EPR-maximum system; human solid tumors run EPR at variable-and-often-low.
metadata.clock: narration (ElevenLabs) — durations below are word-count estimates until GATE 0 audio lock
metadata.color_semantics: TEAL #1F6F5C = mouse xenograft / open EPR / good accumulation; CRIMSON #BF3339 = human desmoplastic tumor / blocked EPR / low accumulation. Never swapped mid-film.
metadata.note: Card exclusions honored: NO BIND-014 trial specifics, NO IFP derivation, NO stromal enzyme or mechanical disruption strategies, NO active vs passive targeting debate. One mechanism only: the mouse model runs EPR at max; patients run EPR at variable-and-often-low.
_variant_todo: rewrite every beat narration_text in the Wonder register (voices/wonder/VOICE.md + MEDHAVY.md) — voice only, facts unchanged
signature tangent 0-1 per video, ONLY on a clear opportunity (see SKILL)
swap the outro to the MEDHAVY outro from AUTHOR.MD :: Medhavy.com
then build audience-namespaced (audio in the new voice, scenes in the new palette)

beats.narration_text: Here's a puzzle worth sitting with. A targeted nanoparticle fills mouse tumors with drug. Give the same particle to patients with the same cancer — and it piles up in the liver instead.

card.copy: Why the Tumor That Shrank in Mice Won't Shrink in Patients

beats.new_visual_element: [TITLE] eyebrow + split title, crimson underline on second line

build.suggested: suggested prompt: [TITLE] eyebrow + split title, crimson underline on second line

beats.narration_text: The preclinical data is striking. A docetaxel nanoparticle accumulates inside a subcutaneous mouse tumor. The mice respond. You would look at that data and think: this is working.

props.headline: In the lab, the result is striking. A docetaxel nanoparticle accumulates…

beats.scene_description: A researcher in a white coat holds up a mouse cage under fluorescent lab light; a glowing tumor visible on the flank of an immunocompromised mouse; cool clinical atmosphere, editorial desaturated treatment
beats.image_prompt: B02, research scientist in white lab coat holding a small mouse cage under bright overhead fluorescent lab lighting, close view showing a small subcutaneous tumor on the mouse's flank glowing with faint fluorescent signal, dark background, cold sterile laboratory, editorial desaturated newsprint treatment, flat print reproduction
beats.new_visual_element: [STILL·ai] mouse in lab, glowing tumor — the preclinical promise

build.suggested: suggested prompt: [STILL·ai] mouse in lab, glowing tumor — the preclinical promise

beats.narration_text: Then the same particle enters a phase two trial. Accumulation across the patient population is far lower and far more variable. Many lesions see almost none. Here's what's actually strange — the chemistry never changed.

production_viz.label: mouse vs patient accumulation bars
production_viz.mechanic: two vertical bars side by side. Left bar (TEAL) tall, labeled 'Mouse xenograft' with value '8% ID/g'. Right bar (CRIMSON) short and broken into scattered marks representing variability, labeled 'Patient tumors' with range '0.1 - 2% ID/g'. Both labeled 'same nanoparticle'.
production_viz.note: numbers are illustrative — labeled as such in FACTCHECK. The key visual is the contrast in height and variability.

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] tall teal bar vs short/variable crimson bars — accumulation contrast

beats.narration_text: A cancer nanoparticle reliably delivered drug to tumors in mice. In patients with the same tumor type, it ended up mostly in the liver. The molecule was identical. We need to figure out why — because the answer changes how you think about the whole field.

card.copy: A cancer nanoparticle reliably delivered drug to tumors in mice. In patients with the same tumor type, it mostly ended up in the liver. The molecule was identical. Why?

beats.new_visual_element: [CARD] THE QUESTION on screen — gap formula

build.suggested: suggested prompt: [CARD] THE QUESTION on screen — gap formula

beats.narration_text: Here is why EPR works when it works. Tumor vasculature is leaky — fenestrations wide enough for nanoparticles to pass through. And lymphatics inside tumors are defective, so particles that enter don't drain away. Together, these let particles accumulate without any active targeting mechanism required.

production_viz.label: EPR schematic — leaky vessel + particle accumulation
production_viz.mechanic: a cross-section of a vessel wall (TEAL rectangle) with gaps in it — fenestrations. Small circles (nanoparticles, TEAL) stream through the gaps into surrounding tissue. A label 'EPR effect' appears. No lymphatic drainage arrow (just the absence of outward flow).
production_viz.note: establish EPR as a real phenomenon before showing why it fails

beats.narration_text: This is the enhanced permeability and retention effect — EPR. It is real. It does work in the laboratory. The interesting part is why: subcutaneous mouse xenografts grow from cancer cell lines under the skin, where vessels are thin-walled, maximally leaky, and the tumor has no dense stroma pressing back.

props.headline: This is the enhanced permeability and retention effect — EPR. It…

beats.scene_description: Cross-sectional diagram-style illustration of a subcutaneous mouse xenograft tumor — thin, fenestrated blood vessels visible, loose extracellular matrix, small nanoparticles (dots) streaming through vessel walls into tumor tissue; schematic, editorial, desaturated
beats.image_prompt: B06, schematic cross-section diagram of a subcutaneous mouse xenograft tumor, showing thin-walled blood vessels with wide gaps (fenestrations), loose extracellular matrix, small circular nanoparticles streaming through vessel walls into tumor parenchyma, clean scientific illustration style, editorial desaturated newsprint treatment, flat print reproduction, no text labels
beats.new_visual_element: [STILL·ai] mouse xenograft cross-section — leaky vessels, loose matrix

build.suggested: suggested prompt: [STILL·ai] mouse xenograft cross-section — leaky vessels, loose matrix

beats.narration_text: Human solid tumors are genuinely different. Pancreatic and prostate cancers grow a dense fibrous scaffold — desmoplastic stroma. It compresses the microvessels from outside. The fenestrations close. EPR stops working. That's not a metaphor; it's a mechanical fact.

production_viz.label: desmoplastic stroma compresses vessels
production_viz.mechanic: a vessel rectangle (CRIMSON-outlined) starts open, then fibrous strands (CRIMSON lines) grow around it, compressing it to near-closure. Nanoparticles approach but cannot enter. Label 'desmoplastic stroma' appears.
production_viz.note: the fibrous compression is the key mechanical image — vessels closing

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] fibrous strands compress vessel cross-section — EPR blocked

beats.narration_text: Here's where it gets strange. Human tumors also maintain high interstitial fluid pressure — a sustained outward flow from inside the tumor. Particles approaching the lesion are physically pushed away before they can accumulate. The tumor is actively defending itself against delivery.

production_viz.label: outward interstitial pressure arrows
production_viz.mechanic: a tumor boundary (CRIMSON circle). Arrows pointing outward from the tumor center, radiating in all directions. Small nanoparticle dots approach from outside but are deflected by the outward arrows. Label 'high interstitial pressure'.
production_viz.note: the outward pressure is an invisible but crucial force — make it visible

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] outward pressure arrows deflecting incoming nanoparticles

beats.narration_text: The mouse model tested nanoparticle delivery under ideal EPR conditions. What patients actually present is EPR at its real-world average — highly variable between lesions, variable between patients, and often far lower than the preclinical model ever suggested it would be.

card.copy: The mouse model runs EPR at maximum. Patients run EPR at variable — and often low.

beats.new_visual_element: [CARD] section card — the core contrast stated

build.suggested: suggested prompt: [CARD] section card — the core contrast stated

beats.narration_text: The interesting part is what failed here. Not EPR itself. The experimental logic. A nanoparticle optimized inside a maximum-EPR system was never actually tested under the conditions it would face in patients. You validated the particle in the wrong world.

production_viz.label: model vs patient EPR spectrum
production_viz.mechanic: A horizontal spectrum bar. Left end labeled 'EPR maximum' (TEAL). Right end labeled 'EPR near-zero' (CRIMSON). A single TEAL dot marks where the mouse model sits — far left. A scatter of CRIMSON dots marks where patients sit — spread across the middle and right. The mouse dot and patient dots have arrows indicating the gap.
production_viz.note: the compare move — the key visual that names the gap

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] EPR spectrum: mouse at maximum, patient scatter across variable range

beats.narration_text: Particles that miss their tumor target don't just disappear — they accumulate in the liver and spleen by default, cleared by the immune cells that patrol those organs. The patient's healthy tissue receives the dose that was meant for the tumor.

production_viz.label: particles routing to liver instead of tumor
production_viz.mechanic: a simple body silhouette outline. Two destinations labeled: 'tumor' (small TEAL circle, off to the side) and 'liver' (large CRIMSON rectangle, prominent). Nanoparticle dots flow in and predominantly go to the liver, with a trickle going to the tumor. Arrows indicate the routing.
production_viz.note: make the liver default visible and concrete

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] body silhouette — particles flood liver, trickle to tumor

beats.narration_text: Let's make the gap concrete. Two tumor cross-sections, side by side. The xenograft: vessel fenestrations 200 nanometers wide — particles stream in, accumulating at 8 percent of injected dose per gram of tissue. These are illustrative numbers, but they capture the right order of magnitude.

production_viz.label: mouse xenograft cross-section — open vessels, particles streaming in
production_viz.mechanic: left half of frame: a schematic tumor cross-section (TEAL border). Vessel lumens shown as open circles with 200 nm label. Nanoparticle dots (TEAL) stream through vessel walls into the tissue. A chip reads '8% ID/g'. Label 'Mouse xenograft' at top.
production_viz.note: 8% ID/g and 200 nm are ILLUSTRATIVE — labeled as such in FACTCHECK

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] left panel: xenograft — wide fenestrations, particles streaming in, 8% ID/g

beats.narration_text: Now the human desmoplastic tumor. Stromal fibers compress the vessels to near-closure. The same particle accumulates at 0.3 percent of injected dose per gram. Same chemistry. Eight times less delivery, across the same diagnosis. Researchers call this interpatient EPR variability — and it's genuinely not what the mouse data prepared anyone to expect.

production_viz.label: human desmoplastic tumor — compressed vessels, particles blocked
production_viz.mechanic: right half of frame (or full frame with split): a schematic tumor cross-section (CRIMSON border). Fibrous stromal lines crowd the vessel lumens, compressing them. Nanoparticle dots (CRIMSON-tinted) pile up outside, blocked. A chip reads '0.3% ID/g'. Label 'Human desmoplastic tumor' at top. A bracket below both panels reads 'same chemistry -- interpatient EPR variability'.
production_viz.note: 0.3% ID/g is ILLUSTRATIVE. 'interpatient EPR variability' is a real research framing — labeled illustrative in context.

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] right panel: human tumor — compressed vessels, particles blocked, 0.3% ID/g

beats.narration_text: The nanoparticle didn't fail. The model did. The subcutaneous xenograft is EPR at maximum — thin walls, no stroma, no outward pressure. The patient's tumor is EPR at its real average — compressed, fibrous, pushing back. Same molecule. Completely different world.

card.copy: The mouse model runs EPR at maximum. The patient runs EPR at real — compressed, fibrous, variable.

build.suggested: suggested prompt: [CARD] endcard — question answered, topic named
